sections.title: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: The CFA Institute Code of Ethics is like a 'professional promise' that all CFA members and candidates make. It's six commitments that guide how you should behave as an investment professional.

WHY THIS MATTERS: Ethics is 15-20% of the CFA Level I exam (27-36 questions out of 180). This is the BIGGEST topic on the exam. Also, if your total score is close to passing, a strong Ethics performance can push you to a PASS. Think of Ethics as your 'safety net' for passing the exam.

THE SIX PROMISES (Code of Ethics):

  1. Act with integrity, competence, diligence, respect, and ethically - Translation: Be honest, know your stuff, work hard, treat people well, and do the right thing. EXAMPLE: If a client asks about an investment you don't understand, say 'I need to research this' rather than making something up. That's acting with competence and integrity.
  2. Place client interests ABOVE your own - Translation: Clients come first, ALWAYS. This is called 'fiduciary duty.' EXAMPLE: You personally own Stock XYZ. You analyze it and realize it's overvalued and should be sold. Even though selling will lose you money personally, you must recommend selling to clients if it's in THEIR best interest. Your loss doesn't matter - client interests come first.
  3. Use reasonable care and independent judgment - Translation: Think for yourself. Don't just copy what others say. EXAMPLE: Your boss recommends buying Company ABC stock. Instead of blindly agreeing, you do your own research. If your analysis says 'sell,' you should say 'sell' even if it disagrees with your boss.
  4. Practice and encourage ethical behavior in others - Translation: Not only must YOU be ethical, but you should also promote ethics around you. EXAMPLE: If you see a colleague doing something questionable, speak up. Don't be a bystander to unethical behavior.
  5. Promote integrity of capital markets - Translation: The investment industry runs on TRUST. Your actions should support that trust. EXAMPLE: Don't participate in schemes that manipulate stock prices, even if they're legal. It undermines market integrity.
  6. Maintain and improve professional competence - Translation: Never stop learning. EXAMPLE: Take continuing education courses, read industry publications, stay current with new regulations and investment products.

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember the acronym 'ICARE' - Integrity, Clients first, Analysis (independent), Respect/encourage others, Education (continuous learning)
keyPoints: Ethics = 15-20% of exam (27-36 questions) - HIGHEST weighted topic
Strong Ethics performance can help borderline candidates PASS
Six components of Code of Ethics - memorize all six
Client interests come BEFORE personal AND employer interests (fiduciary duty)
Exercise INDEPENDENT judgment - don't blindly follow others
Must promote ethical behavior among colleagues, not just practice it yourself
Code applies to ALL professional activities, not just investment decisions
When in doubt, ask: 'Would I be comfortable if this appeared in the news?'
Memory aid: 'ICARE' - Integrity, Clients first, Analysis, Respect, Education

practiceQuestions.question: According to the CFA Institute Code of Ethics, when a member's personal investment conflicts with a client's interest, the member should:
options: Balance both interests equally to find a fair middle ground
Place the client's interests above their own personal interests
Disclose the conflict to the client and then proceed as planned
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Code of Ethics requires members to place CLIENT interests ABOVE their own personal interests. This is the fiduciary duty. Simply disclosing the conflict (option C) is not enough - you must actually prioritize the client. 'Balancing' interests (option A) is wrong because client interests must come FIRST, not be treated equally. Think of it this way: If you were the client, wouldn't you want your advisor putting YOUR interests first?

practiceQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the six components of the Code of Ethics?
options: Maintain and improve professional competence through continuous learning
Guarantee investment returns to clients based on past performance
Promote the integrity and viability of the global capital markets
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Code of Ethics does NOT permit guaranteeing returns - this is actually PROHIBITED. Investment returns can NEVER be guaranteed because markets are uncertain. Guaranteeing returns would violate Standard I(C) on Misrepresentation. Options A and C are both actual components of the Code. EXAM TIP: If an answer choice mentions 'guaranteeing returns,' it's almost always wrong on the CFA exam.

practiceQuestions.question: A CFA charterholder teaches finance courses at a local university on weekends. The Standards of Professional Conduct:
options: Do not apply because teaching is not investment work
Apply only if students are paying for the courses
Apply to the teaching activities because it is professional conduct
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Standards apply to ALL professional activities where you represent yourself as a CFA member or finance professional, including teaching. Even though teaching is not direct investment work, you are still acting as an investment professional. Your behavior as a teacher reflects on the CFA designation and the investment industry. EXAM TIP: When in doubt, ask 'Am I acting in a professional capacity?' If yes, the Standards apply.

sections.title: Professional Misconduct - Standard I(D)
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Standard I(D) says 'don't be dishonest' - in your work AND in your personal life. This standard is broader than many people expect.

THE KEY TEST: Would a reasonable person who knows about your conduct question whether you're fit to manage their money? If yes, it's probably misconduct.

WHAT COUNTS AS MISCONDUCT:

  • Dishonesty: Lying, cheating, stealing, deception. EXAMPLE: Lying to a client about an investment's performance.
  • Fraud: Intentional deception for personal gain. EXAMPLE: Creating fake documents to hide investment losses.
  • Conduct reflecting poorly on your reputation: Any behavior that makes people question your integrity. EXAMPLE: Being convicted of theft, even from a non-work situation.

CRITICAL POINT: This applies to BOTH work AND personal life. You can violate this standard for something you do on weekends, completely unrelated to work.

REAL EXAMPLES:

  • ✅ VIOLATION: Convicted of embezzling money from a charity (involves dishonesty)
  • ✅ VIOLATION: Caught cheating on the CFA exam (involves dishonesty)
  • ✅ VIOLATION: Arrested for shoplifting (involves theft/dishonesty)
  • ❌ NOT A VIOLATION: Single speeding ticket (minor traffic violation)
  • ❌ NOT A VIOLATION: Personal bankruptcy due to medical bills (no dishonesty involved)
  • ❌ NOT A VIOLATION: Divorce proceedings (personal matter, no dishonesty)

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Ask yourself: 'Does this involve DISHONESTY?' If yes → violation. If it's just a minor personal issue with no dishonesty → probably not a violation.
keyPoints: Standard applies to BOTH professional AND personal conduct
The test: Would reasonable people question your fitness to serve clients?
Dishonesty, fraud, deceit = ALWAYS violations
Criminal convictions involving fraud typically violate this standard
Minor traffic violations (speeding, parking) = typically NOT violations
Personal bankruptcy alone = NOT a violation (unless fraud involved)
Cheating on ANY professional exam = violation
Multiple minor violations can accumulate to become a problem
Memory aid: 'DISHONESTY' = the key word for this standard

practiceQuestions.question: A CFA member is convicted of shoplifting items worth $50 from a store. This conduct:
options: Does not violate the Standards because it is unrelated to work
Violates Standard I(D) because it involves dishonesty
Only violates the Standards if the member lies about it
practiceQuestions.explanation: Shoplifting involves theft and dishonesty, which violates Standard I(D). Even though this occurred outside of work and is unrelated to investments, the standard applies to personal conduct too. A reasonable client might question whether someone who steals from stores can be trusted with their investment portfolio. EXAM TIP: Crimes involving dishonesty (theft, fraud, embezzlement) almost always violate Standard I(D), regardless of where they occur.

practiceQuestions.question: Which of the following would MOST LIKELY violate Standard I(D)?
options: A single conviction for driving under the influence (DUI)
Filing for personal bankruptcy due to medical expenses
Cheating on a professional licensing examination
practiceQuestions.explanation: Cheating on ANY professional examination involves dishonesty and directly violates Standard I(D). Option A (single DUI) typically does not violate the standard unless there are aggravating factors involving dishonesty. Option B (personal bankruptcy) alone does not violate the standard - bankruptcy is a legal process and doesn't inherently involve dishonesty. EXAM TIP: Look for the answer that involves DISHONESTY - that's usually the violation.

sections.title: Knowledge of the Law - Standard I(A)
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: You must know and follow the laws that apply to your work. You can't say 'I didn't know that was illegal' as an excuse.

THE GOLDEN RULE: When local law and CFA Standards conflict, follow whichever is MORE STRICT (more demanding).

HOW TO HANDLE CONFLICTS:

SituationWhat to FollowExample
Law is MORE strict than StandardsFollow the LAWLaw requires 10 years record retention, Standards require 7 years → Keep records for 10 years
Standards are MORE strict than lawFollow the STANDARDSLaw allows gifts up to $500, Standards require disclosure of ALL gifts → Disclose all gifts
Law and Standards are the sameFollow either (same thing)Both prohibit insider trading → Don't engage in insider trading

IF YOU DISCOVER ILLEGAL ACTIVITY AT YOUR FIRM:

  1. Step 1: Do NOT participate in the illegal activity
  2. Step 2: Tell your supervisor or compliance department (in writing - email is good)
  3. Step 3: If they don't fix it, distance yourself from the activity
  4. Step 4: If still not fixed, consider reporting to regulators
  5. Step 5: In extreme cases, consider quitting your job

REAL EXAMPLE: You work in Country X where accepting client gifts up to $500 is legal with no disclosure required. CFA Standards require disclosure of ALL gifts. What do you do? ANSWER: Disclose all gifts. The Standards are MORE STRICT, so you follow them. Even though local law is more lenient, as a CFA member/candidate you must follow the stricter rule.

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'STRICTER WINS' - whichever rule is more strict is the one you follow.
keyPoints: Must know and comply with ALL applicable laws - ignorance is not an excuse
When law and Standards conflict, follow the MORE STRICT requirement
Cannot knowingly participate in illegal activities under any circumstances
Document objections in writing (email, memo to file)
May need to report to regulators if firm doesn't fix violations
In extreme cases, may need to resign to avoid association
Applies to laws in ALL jurisdictions where you do business
Memory aid: 'STRICTER WINS' - follow the more strict rule

practiceQuestions.question: A CFA member works in a country where local law permits accepting gifts from clients up to $1,000 without disclosure. The CFA Standards require disclosure of all gifts regardless of value. The member should:
options: Follow local law and not disclose gifts under $1,000
Follow CFA Standards and disclose all gifts
Follow local law but disclose gifts over $500
practiceQuestions.explanation: The CFA Standards are MORE STRICT (require disclosure of ALL gifts vs. only gifts over $1,000). When Standards are stricter than local law, members must follow the Standards. This is a direct application of Standard I(A). EXAM TIP: This is a VERY common exam scenario - local law is lenient, Standards are strict. Always choose the Standards in these cases.

practiceQuestions.question: A member discovers their supervisor is engaging in illegal insider trading. The member should FIRST:
options: Ignore it since the supervisor is responsible
Disassociate from the activity and report to compliance in writing
Immediately contact the media to expose the wrongdoing
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard I(A) requires members to NOT participate in illegal activities. The first step is to disassociate and report through proper channels (compliance, legal) IN WRITING. This creates documentation that you objected. Option A is wrong - ignoring violations makes you complicit. Option C is wrong - media is not the appropriate first step; use internal channels and regulators first. EXAM TIP: 'In writing' is key - always document your objections.

sections.title: Independence and Objectivity - Standard I(B)
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: You must think for yourself and not let others influence your investment decisions. Your analysis and recommendations must be YOURS, not what someone else wants to hear.

COMMON SITUATIONS WHERE THIS APPLIES:

  • Gifts from clients or companies: Small gifts (coffee mug, calendar) = OK. Expensive gifts (tickets to Super Bowl, luxury vacation) = NOT OK without permission. EXAMPLE: A company whose stock you cover invites you to a $5,000-per-person resort weekend. You must decline OR get employer permission AND disclose it. Why? Because this could influence your analysis of their stock.
  • Pressure from investment banking: Your firm's banking division is helping Company XYZ raise money. They ask you to issue a 'buy' recommendation on XYZ stock. Your analysis says 'sell.' What do you do? ANSWER: Issue the 'sell' recommendation. Banking pressure cannot influence your analysis.
  • Issuer-paid research: A company pays you to write a research report on them. This is allowed ONLY IF your compensation is a FLAT FEE (not tied to your conclusions). You must disclose that the research was paid for by the company. EXAMPLE: Company ABC pays you $10,000 to write a report. You can accept IF: (1) you get $10,000 regardless of whether you say 'buy' or 'sell', and (2) you clearly disclose 'This research was paid for by Company ABC.'

THE KEY TEST: Would a reasonable person think this gift or relationship could influence your judgment? If yes, don't accept it (or get permission and disclose).

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'GIFT' - Gifts should be Insufficient to influence, Fully disclosed, Permission obtained, Trivial in value
keyPoints: Must maintain independent judgment in all investment analysis
Cannot accept gifts that could compromise independence
Modest gifts (under $100) typically acceptable without disclosure
Expensive gifts require employer permission AND disclosure
Investment banking cannot pressure research analysts
Issuer-paid research allowed only with flat fee (not performance-based)
Must disclose any conflicts of interest to clients and employers
Memory aid: 'GIFT' - Gifts Insufficient to influence, Fully disclosed, Permission, Trivial

practiceQuestions.question: A research analyst receives two tickets worth $800 to a professional sports game from a company whose stock she covers. She should:
options: Accept the tickets without telling anyone since they're a gift
Decline the tickets or seek employer permission before accepting
Accept the tickets and sell them for cash instead
practiceQuestions.explanation: Tickets worth $800 are NOT a modest gift - they could reasonably be expected to influence the analyst's judgment. She must either decline them OR seek employer permission before accepting. Selling the tickets for cash (option C) is actually WORSE because it converts the gift to cash, which looks like a bribe. EXAM TIP: When gifts are from companies you cover/review, be EXTRA careful. The appearance of impropriety matters.

practiceQuestions.question: An investment banking client asks a research analyst to issue a 'buy' recommendation on their stock. The analyst's analysis indicates the stock should be 'sold.' The analyst should:
options: Issue the 'buy' recommendation to maintain the banking relationship
Issue the 'sell' recommendation based on independent analysis
Issue a 'hold' recommendation as a compromise
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard I(B) requires independence and objectivity. The analyst must issue the recommendation that reflects their TRUE analysis, regardless of pressure from investment banking. Option A violates the standard by letting banking pressure influence research. Option C is also wrong - a 'hold' recommendation when analysis says 'sell' is still not independent. EXAM TIP: Never compromise your analysis to please banking clients, employers, or anyone else.

sections.title: Misrepresentation - Standard I(C)
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Don't lie, don't exaggerate, and don't steal other people's work. Be honest and give credit where credit is due.

THREE MAIN TYPES OF MISREPRESENTATION:

  1. Guaranteeing Returns (NEVER ALLOWED): You can NEVER guarantee or promise specific investment returns. EXAMPLES OF VIOLATIONS: 'I guarantee you'll earn 12% this year.' 'This investment is risk-free.' 'You won't lose money with this strategy.' WHY IT'S WRONG: Investment returns are NEVER certain. Markets go up and down. Even 'safe' investments like government bonds have some risk. Making guarantees is dishonest.
  2. Plagiarism (Stealing Others' Work): Using someone else's work, ideas, or words without giving them credit. EXAMPLES OF VIOLATIONS: Copying a colleague's research report and putting your name on it. Using an analyst's recommendation from another firm without saying where it came from. Downloading charts from the internet and using them in your presentation without attribution. EXAMPLES OF NOT PLAGIARISM: Using factual data from recognized sources (government statistics, stock exchange data). Ideas that are 'common knowledge' in the industry. Work you created yourself (obviously!).
  3. Misrepresenting Your Qualifications or Track Record: Lying about your credentials, experience, or past performance. EXAMPLES OF VIOLATIONS: Claiming you have a CFA charter when you're only a candidate. Exaggerating your investment returns ('I earned 50%' when you actually earned 15%). Claiming you 'saved' a client money when you can't prove it.

REAL EXAMPLE: You're pitching to a potential client. You say: 'I guarantee this portfolio will earn 10% annually with no risk.' This violates Standard I(C) TWICE: (1) guaranteeing returns, and (2) implying investments can be risk-free. Even if you genuinely believe this, it's misrepresentation.

HOW TO AVOID PLAGIARISM:

  • When in doubt, cite your source
  • Use quotation marks for direct quotes
  • Attribute ideas even when paraphrasing
  • For data: if it's from a recognized statistical source (government, exchange), no attribution needed. If it's from another analyst's report, attribution needed.

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'NO LIES' - No guarantees, Own your work (don't plagiarize), Identify your sources, be Truthful about qualifications
keyPoints: NEVER guarantee investment returns - this is always a violation
Cannot claim investments are 'risk-free' or 'guaranteed'
Must attribute others' work, ideas, and direct quotes
Factual data from recognized sources doesn't need attribution
Cannot misrepresent qualifications, credentials, or track record
Plagiarism applies to written work, oral presentations, and electronic media
When in doubt about attribution, cite the source
Memory aid: 'NO LIES' - No guarantees, Own your work, Identify sources

practiceQuestions.question: A portfolio manager tells a prospective client: 'Based on my analysis, I'm confident this strategy will generate 15% returns annually with minimal risk.' This statement:
options: Is acceptable because it's based on thorough analysis
Violates Standard I(C) because it implies a guarantee of returns
Is acceptable if the manager has achieved 15% returns in the past
practiceQuestions.explanation: This statement violates Standard I(C) because it implies a guarantee of specific returns ('will generate 15%'). Even though the manager says 'based on my analysis,' the statement still suggests certainty about future performance, which is misrepresentation. Past performance (option C) does NOT justify implying future guarantees. EXAM TIP: Watch for words like 'will,' 'guarantee,' 'certain,' 'assured' - these often signal violations.

practiceQuestions.question: An analyst reads a research report from another firm and uses the key conclusions in her own report without attribution. This:
options: Is acceptable because research conclusions are not copyrightable
Violates Standard I(C) as plagiarism
Is acceptable if she changes the wording
practiceQuestions.explanation: Using someone else's conclusions without attribution is plagiarism, which violates Standard I(C). It doesn't matter if she changes the wording - the IDEAS came from someone else and must be attributed. Option A is wrong - while conclusions may not be copyrightable in a legal sense, using them without attribution is still plagiarism under the Standards. EXAM TIP: Plagiarism isn't just about copying words - it's about using others' IDEAS without credit.

sections.title: Duties to Clients - Loyalty, Prudence, and Care (III-A)
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: You work for your CLIENTS, not yourself or your employer. Put their interests first, always. This is called 'fiduciary duty' - it's the highest standard of care in the investment industry.

THREE KEY WORDS:

  • Loyalty: Be faithful to clients. Their interests come first.
  • Prudence: Be careful and thoughtful. Act like a 'prudent person' would with their own money.
  • Care: Put in the effort. Do the research. Don't be careless with client money.

WHO IS YOUR CLIENT? This is important and sometimes tricky:

  • If you manage a pension fund → The pension fund is your client (NOT the individual employees)
  • If you manage a mutual fund → The mutual fund is your client (NOT the individual shareholders)
  • If you work directly with individuals → The individual is your client

KEY REQUIREMENTS:

  1. Vote proxies in clients' best interests: When companies have shareholder votes, you must vote in ways that benefit clients. Don't just automatically vote with management. Don't waste proxy votes - they belong to clients.
  2. Seek best execution: When trading for clients, get the best possible price. Don't trade just to generate commissions ('churning').
  3. Disclose conflicts: If you have any personal interest that could conflict with client interests, tell the client. EXAMPLE: You recommend a stock that your spouse owns. Disclose this to the client.
  4. Soft dollars: Client brokerage commissions belong to CLIENTS. You can only use them for research or services that benefit clients. Can't use client commissions to pay for your office rent or personal expenses.

REAL EXAMPLE: You manage a pension fund. Company XYZ is having a shareholder vote on whether to give the CEO a huge bonus. Your analysis says the bonus is excessive and not in shareholders' interests. The pension fund's management says 'vote with the CEO.' What do you do? ANSWER: Vote against the bonus. Your duty is to the pension fund beneficiaries (the ultimate clients), not the pension fund management. Voting against excessive compensation is in clients' best interests.

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'CLIENTS FIRST' - Client interests, Loyalty, Independent judgment, Execute best, Never waste proxy votes, Trust is everything, Invest carefully, Serve faithfully, First always
keyPoints: Client interests come BEFORE personal AND employer interests
Must exercise prudence and care (the 'prudent person' standard)
Must vote proxies in clients' best interests, not management's
Must seek best execution for client transactions
Must disclose ALL conflicts of interest to clients
Understand who your client is (entity vs. beneficiaries)
Soft dollars (client commissions) can only be used for client-benefiting research
Cannot 'churn' accounts (excessive trading to generate commissions)
Memory aid: 'CLIENTS FIRST' - Client interests come first, always

practiceQuestions.question: When managing a pension fund portfolio, the CFA member's client is:
options: The pension fund management
The pension fund beneficiaries (employees)
The company that sponsors the pension plan
practiceQuestions.explanation: The ultimate clients are the pension fund beneficiaries (the employees who will receive pension benefits). While you work with pension fund management, your fiduciary duty is to the beneficiaries. This is important when there are conflicts - for example, if management wants you to vote proxies one way but it's not in beneficiaries' interests, you must vote for beneficiaries. EXAM TIP: For pooled investments (pension funds, mutual funds, endowments), the beneficiaries/shareholders are the ultimate clients.

practiceQuestions.question: Regarding proxy voting, a member should:
options: Always vote with company management to maintain good relationships
Vote proxies in the best interests of clients
Not vote proxies as it creates potential conflicts
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(A) requires members to vote proxies in the BEST INTERESTS OF CLIENTS. This means analyzing each proposal and voting accordingly. Automatically voting with management (option A) is a violation because it puts the company's interests ahead of clients'. Not voting at all (option C) is also a violation - proxy votes are client assets and wasting them violates the duty of care. EXAM TIP: Proxy voting is a fiduciary responsibility - you MUST vote, and you MUST vote for clients' benefit.

sections.title: Fair Dealing - Standard III(B)
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Treat all clients FAIRLY when making recommendations or taking investment action. This doesn't mean treating everyone EXACTLY the same, but it does mean not favoring some clients over others.

FAIR vs. EQUAL - IMPORTANT DISTINCTION:

  • FAIR dealing (required): All clients should have a reasonable opportunity to act on your recommendations. Don't tell some clients before others.
  • EQUAL treatment (NOT required): You CAN offer different service levels to different clients. EXAMPLE: Premium clients might get monthly reports and weekly calls. Basic clients might get quarterly reports only. This is OK as long as it's DISCLOSED in advance and clients agree to it.

WHAT'S NOT FAIR (VIOLATIONS):

  • Telling your favorite clients about a stock recommendation BEFORE telling other clients
  • Buying a stock for your own account before buying it for clients who want it ('front-running')
  • Allocating profitable trades to favored clients and unprofitable trades to others
  • Having a 'tiered' system where some clients get recommendations days before others

WHAT IS FAIR (ALLOWED):

  • Different fee structures for different service levels (if disclosed)
  • Different minimum investment amounts for different share classes
  • Broad dissemination of recommendations (email to all clients at once, posting on website)
  • Tiered service levels (premium vs. basic) as long as all clients know what they're getting

BEST PRACTICES TO ENSURE FAIR DEALING:

  1. Disseminate recommendations broadly (email blast, website posting, etc.)
  2. Don't tell some clients before others
  3. Execute trades for all clients at approximately the same time
  4. Use a systematic approach to allocate trades (pro-rata, not favoritism)
  5. Document your trade allocation procedures

REAL EXAMPLE: You're about to issue a 'buy' recommendation on Stock ABC. You have 100 clients. What's the FAIR approach? ANSWER: Send the recommendation to all 100 clients at approximately the same time (same email blast, same time). Don't call your 10 biggest clients first, then email the rest later. That's favoritism and violates Standard III(B).

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'FAIR' - Fair opportunity for all, All interested clients notified, Information released simultaneously, Relationships disclosed
keyPoints: Must treat all clients FAIRLY (not necessarily EQUALLY)
Cannot favor some clients over others for recommendations
Different service levels OK if disclosed to all clients in advance
Disseminate recommendations to all interested clients SIMULTANEOUSLY
Don't tell favored clients before other clients
Trade allocation must be systematic (pro-rata), not based on favoritism
Front-running (trading for yourself before clients) is prohibited
Memory aid: 'FAIR' - Fair opportunity, All notified, Information simultaneous, Relationships disclosed

practiceQuestions.question: A firm is about to issue a 'buy' recommendation on a stock. To comply with Standard III(B) on Fair Dealing, the firm should:
options: Tell their largest clients first, then other clients 24 hours later
Make the recommendation available to all clients who might be interested at approximately the same time
Only tell clients who have accounts over $1 million
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(B) requires fair dealing - all clients who might be interested should have a reasonable opportunity to receive and act on recommendations at approximately the same time. Option A violates the standard by favoring large clients. Option C discriminates against smaller clients. EXAM TIP: 'Simultaneous' or 'approximately the same time' is the key phrase for fair dealing questions.

practiceQuestions.question: Standard III(B) on Fair Dealing allows for:
options: Different service levels for different clients if disclosed in advance
Telling some clients about recommendations before others based on account size
Charging all clients the same fees regardless of service level
practiceQuestions.explanation: Fair dealing does NOT mean identical treatment. Different service levels (and fees) are acceptable as long as they are DISCLOSED to all clients in advance and clients agree to them. Option B violates fair dealing by favoring some clients. Option C is not required - different service levels can have different fees. EXAM TIP: The key is DISCLOSURE. If clients know what they're getting (and what they're not getting) before they sign up, different service levels are acceptable.

sections.title: Suitability - Standard III(C)
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Before recommending an investment to a client, you must understand that client's situation. Don't recommend the same thing to everyone. A 25-year-old with high risk tolerance has different needs than a 70-year-old retiree.

WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW ABOUT EACH CLIENT (The 'IPS' - Investment Policy Statement):

  • Return objectives: What return does the client need? (e.g., 'I need 7% annually to meet my retirement goal')
  • Risk tolerance: How much volatility can they handle emotionally and financially? (e.g., 'I can't sleep when my portfolio drops 10%')
  • Time horizon: When will they need the money? (e.g., 'I need this for college in 5 years' vs. 'This is for retirement in 30 years')
  • Liquidity needs: Will they need to access the money soon? (e.g., 'I might need $50,000 for a house down payment in 2 years')
  • Tax situation: What's their tax bracket? Taxable or tax-deferred account?
  • Legal/regulatory constraints: Any restrictions? (e.g., 'I can't invest in tobacco stocks for religious reasons')
  • Unique circumstances: Anything else relevant? (e.g., 'I work for Company XYZ so I already have lots of XYZ stock in my 401k')

KEY PRINCIPLE: Judge suitability in the context of the CLIENT'S ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not individual investments in isolation.

REAL EXAMPLE: Your client is a 30-year-old software engineer with stable income, no debt, $500,000 portfolio, and 35 years until retirement. She asks about investing 10% of her portfolio in a speculative biotech startup. Is this suitable? ANSWER: Probably YES. Even though the biotech stock is very risky, it's only 10% of her portfolio, she has a long time horizon, and she can afford the risk. The same recommendation would be UNSUITABLE for a 70-year-old retiree with limited savings.

WHAT IF A CLIENT REQUESTS AN UNSUITABLE INVESTMENT?

  1. Explain why it's not suitable for their situation
  2. Document your explanation (in writing)
  3. If they insist, you may need to decline the request
  4. In extreme cases, consider terminating the relationship

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'RRTTLL' - Return, Risk, Time horizon, Taxes, Liquidity, Legal (the six components of an IPS)
keyPoints: Must understand client's objectives and constraints BEFORE recommending
Must assess client's risk tolerance (emotional and financial)
Must consider time horizon, liquidity needs, tax situation
Judge suitability in context of ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not individual investments
Must update client information REGULARLY (at least annually)
If client requests unsuitable investment, explain and document
Apply to BOTH recommendations AND investment actions
Memory aid: 'RRTTLL' - Return, Risk, Time, Taxes, Liquidity, Legal

practiceQuestions.question: Before making an investment recommendation, a member must make a reasonable inquiry into all of the following EXCEPT:
options: The client's risk tolerance and return objectives
The client's favorite industry sectors
The client's time horizon and liquidity needs
practiceQuestions.explanation: While client preferences about industries may be noted, they are NOT required for suitability determination. The REQUIRED elements are risk tolerance, return objectives, time horizon, liquidity needs, tax situation, and legal constraints. A client's 'favorite' sectors are preferences, not suitability requirements. EXAM TIP: Know the difference between what's REQUIRED for suitability (RRTTLL) vs. what's merely a preference.

practiceQuestions.question: A 70-year-old retiree with a $200,000 portfolio asks the advisor to invest 50% in a speculative cryptocurrency. The advisor believes this is unsuitable. The advisor should:
options: Make the investment because the client requested it
Explain why it's unsuitable, document the explanation, and decline if the client insists
Invest 25% as a compromise
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(C) requires recommendations to be suitable for the client's situation. A 70-year-old retiree with limited assets should not have 50% in speculative investments. The advisor must explain why it's unsuitable, document this explanation, and if the client insists, decline the request. Option A violates the standard by making an unsuitable investment. Option C is also unsuitable - compromising on an unsuitable recommendation is still a violation. EXAM TIP: Never make an unsuitable investment, even if the client requests it. Your professional judgment matters.

sections.title: Performance Presentation - Standard III(D)
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: When you tell clients or prospects about your investment performance, be honest, complete, and fair. Don't cherry-pick only your best results.

WHAT'S PROHIBITED (VIOLATIONS):

  • Cherry-picking: Showing only your best-performing accounts while hiding poor performers. EXAMPLE: You manage 20 accounts. Five did great (+30%), fifteen did poorly (-5%). You create a marketing brochure showing only the five best accounts. This is MISREPRESENTATION.
  • Imputing past performance: Claiming your new fund has the same performance as your old fund (when they have different strategies or you didn't manage the old fund). EXAMPLE: You just started Fund ABC. You tell clients: 'This fund will perform like my previous fund which earned 15% annually.' Unless you can prove the strategies are identical AND you managed the previous fund, this is misrepresentation.
  • Presenting gross returns without disclosure: Showing returns BEFORE fees without clearly saying 'gross of fees.' Clients care about what they actually earn (net of fees).
  • Using hypothetical performance without labeling: Showing 'simulated' or 'backtested' results without clearly saying it's hypothetical (not actual performance).

WHAT'S REQUIRED (BEST PRACTICES):

  • Present COMPOSITE performance (group similar accounts together)
  • Clearly disclose whether performance is gross or net of fees
  • Clearly label any hypothetical or simulated performance
  • Include relevant benchmarks for comparison
  • Follow GIPS (Global Investment Performance Standards) when possible

REAL EXAMPLE: You're pitching to a potential client. You say: 'My average annual return is 12%.' What's wrong with this? ANSWER: Several things: (1) Is this gross or net of fees? (2) Over what time period? (3) Is this one account or a composite? (4) What was the risk/volatility? A proper presentation would be: 'Our balanced composite returned 12% annually (net of fees) over the 10-year period ending December 2024, with a standard deviation of 8%.'

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'HONEST' - Honest presentation, No cherry-picking, All relevant disclosures, Simulated performance labeled, Every account included in composites, Transparent about fees
keyPoints: Cannot misrepresent past or expected performance
Must present performance FAIRLY, ACCURATELY, and COMPLETELY
Cannot cherry-pick only best-performing accounts
Must disclose if performance is gross or net of fees
Must clearly label hypothetical or simulated performance
Should present COMPOSITE performance (not selected accounts)
Should follow GIPS (Global Investment Performance Standards) when possible
Memory aid: 'HONEST' - Honest, No cherry-picking, All disclosures, Simulated labeled, Every account, Transparent

practiceQuestions.question: A portfolio manager creates a marketing brochure showing only the five best-performing accounts out of 50 total accounts managed. This practice:
options: Is acceptable marketing as long as the returns are accurate
Violates Standard III(D) on Performance Presentation
Is required by regulations
practiceQuestions.explanation: Cherry-picking only the best accounts misrepresents overall performance and violates Standard III(D). Performance should be presented for COMPOSITES (groups of similar accounts), not selected accounts. Even if the returns shown are accurate, presenting only the best accounts is misleading because it doesn't represent typical client experience. EXAM TIP: If you see 'selected accounts' or 'representative accounts' in an answer choice, it's usually a violation.

practiceQuestions.question: When presenting investment performance that includes hypothetical or backtested results, members must:
options: Present it the same as actual performance
Clearly label it as hypothetical and not actual performance
Not present hypothetical performance under any circumstances
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(D) permits presenting hypothetical or backtested performance, but it MUST be clearly labeled as such. Clients and prospects need to understand that hypothetical performance is not actual results and may not reflect real-world trading. Option A is wrong because hypothetical and actual performance must be distinguished. Option C is wrong - hypothetical performance is allowed if properly disclosed. EXAM TIP: 'Hypothetical' or 'simulated' performance is OK with disclosure, but never present it as if it were actual performance.

sections.title: Confidentiality - Standard III(E)
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Keep client information PRIVATE. Don't share it with others unless the client says it's OK or the law requires it.

WHAT INFORMATION IS CONFIDENTIAL?

  • Personal information (name, address, Social Security number, etc.)
  • Financial information (income, net worth, account balances, etc.)
  • Investment holdings and transactions
  • Estate planning information
  • Tax returns and tax information
  • ANY non-public information about the client

WHO DOES THIS PROTECT?

  • Current clients
  • Former clients (yes, even after they leave you)
  • Prospective clients (even if they never become clients)

WHEN CAN YOU DISCLOSE CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION?

  1. Client gives permission: Get it IN WRITING. EXAMPLE: Client wants you to talk to their accountant about tax planning. Get written authorization specifying what information can be shared and with whom.
  2. Information concerns illegal activities: If you discover client is doing something illegal (money laundering, tax fraud, etc.), you MAY disclose to authorities. You're not REQUIRED to disclose (unless local law requires it), but you're PERMITTED to.
  3. Required by law: If a court orders you to disclose, or if law requires reporting (like suspicious activity reports for money laundering).
  4. To defend yourself: If a client complains about you to regulators or sues you, you can disclose confidential information as needed to defend yourself.

REAL EXAMPLE: A prospective client meets with you to discuss managing their portfolio. They share detailed financial information. They decide not to hire you. Can you tell other people about their finances? ANSWER: NO. Standard III(E) protects prospective clients too. Just because they didn't become clients doesn't mean you can share their information.

REAL EXAMPLE: A former client left your firm 2 years ago. A colleague asks you about this person's financial situation. Can you tell them? ANSWER: NO. Confidentiality continues even after the client relationship ends. The information is still confidential.

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'SECRET' - Secure information, Exceptions permitted (permission, illegal, required by law, to defend), Continues after relationship ends, Everyone protected (current, former, prospective), Required to protect confidentiality, Trust is essential
keyPoints: Must maintain client confidentiality ALWAYS
Protects current, former, AND prospective clients
Can disclose if client gives WRITTEN permission
Can disclose information about ILLEGAL activities
Can disclose if required by law or court order
Can disclose to defend against client allegations
Confidentiality continues after client relationship ends
Memory aid: 'SECRET' - Secure, Exceptions, Continues, Everyone, Required, Trust

practiceQuestions.question: A member may disclose confidential client information without client permission when:
options: The information concerns illegal activities by the client
A competing firm asks for the information for marketing purposes
It would help the member attract new clients
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(E) permits (but doesn't require) disclosure when the information concerns illegal activities. Options B and C are never acceptable reasons to disclose confidential information. Competitor requests and marketing purposes are NOT exceptions to the confidentiality rule. EXAM TIP: The four exceptions are: (1) client permission, (2) illegal activities, (3) required by law, (4) to defend yourself. If it's not one of these four, don't disclose.

practiceQuestions.question: Client confidentiality under Standard III(E) applies to:
options: Only current clients
Current and former clients only
Current, former, AND prospective clients
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(E) protects the confidentiality of information about current, former, AND prospective clients. The duty continues even after the client relationship ends. EXAM TIP: Many candidates forget that prospective clients are protected. Even if someone meets with you once and never becomes a client, their information is still confidential.

sections.title: Duties to Employers - Standard IV
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: You have duties to your employer too, not just clients. Be loyal, don't steal, and if you're a supervisor, make sure your team follows the rules.

THREE PARTS OF STANDARD IV:

  1. IV(A) - Loyalty to Employer:
    • Act for your employer's benefit
    • Don't misappropriate employer's property or clients
    • Don't solicit clients before leaving your firm
    • Can prepare to compete (get licenses, rent office) but can't actually compete while employed
    • When leaving: can't take client lists, proprietary information, or computer files
  2. IV(B) - Additional Compensation:
    • Can't accept gifts/benefits from third parties that create conflicts
    • If offered additional compensation (bonus, gifts, etc.), must disclose to employer AND get permission
    • EXAMPLE: A client offers you a $5,000 bonus for good performance. You must tell your employer and get permission before accepting.
  3. IV(C) - Responsibilities of Supervisors:
    • Must make reasonable efforts to detect and prevent violations
    • Must have adequate compliance procedures
    • If you discover violations, must take action to stop them
    • Can delegate compliance tasks but NOT responsibility

REAL EXAMPLE - LOYALTY: You're thinking about leaving your firm to start your own business. What can you do while still employed? CAN DO: Register for the CFA exam, rent office space for your future business, create a business plan. CANNOT DO: Solicit your current firm's clients, use firm resources for your new business, take client lists or proprietary information.

REAL EXAMPLE - SUPERVISOR RESPONSIBILITIES: You're a supervisor. One of your team members is doing something that might violate the Standards. What must you do? ANSWER: (1) Investigate to confirm, (2) If violation confirmed, stop it immediately, (3) Put procedures in place to prevent recurrence, (4) Document everything. You can't ignore it or delegate the responsibility to someone else.

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'LOYAL' - Loyal to employer, Offered compensation must be disclosed, Your team's violations are your responsibility (if supervisor), Act for employer's benefit, Leave properly (no stealing clients/property)
keyPoints: Must act for employer's benefit in all professional activities
Cannot accept additional compensation without employer permission
Cannot misappropriate employer's property, clients, or confidential information
When leaving firm, can prepare to compete but not actually compete
Supervisors must make reasonable efforts to prevent violations
Supervisors can delegate tasks but NOT responsibility for compliance
Must have adequate compliance procedures if you're a supervisor
Memory aid: 'LOYAL' - Loyal, Offered compensation disclosed, Your team's violations, Act for employer, Leave properly

practiceQuestions.question: A supervisor discovers a subordinate has violated the Code and Standards. The supervisor should:
options: Ignore it if no clients were harmed
Take reasonable steps to address the violation and prevent recurrence
Immediately terminate the employee without investigation
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard IV(C) requires supervisors to make reasonable efforts to detect and prevent violations. When violations are discovered, supervisors must take action to address them and prevent recurrence. Option A is wrong - ignoring violations makes the supervisor complicit. Option C is wrong - immediate termination without investigation may not be appropriate; the response should be proportional to the violation. EXAM TIP: Supervisors have AFFIRMATIVE DUTY to address violations. Ignoring them is itself a violation.

practiceQuestions.question: Before accepting a $10,000 bonus from a client, a member should:
options: Accept it without telling anyone since it's from a client
Disclose it to their employer and get written permission before accepting
Decline all client bonuses regardless of circumstances
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard IV(B) requires members to disclose any additional compensation arrangements to their employer and obtain permission BEFORE accepting. The bonus must be disclosed in writing, and employer must give written permission. Option A violates the standard by not disclosing. Option C is not required - bonuses are allowed with proper disclosure and permission. EXAM TIP: 'Additional compensation' includes bonuses, gifts, benefits - anything of value beyond normal compensation.
